sample.promise: A decision-ready public service delivery publication for Kenya, built from traceable synthetic evidence.
sample.executive_story: What follows is a governed account of public service delivery performance in Kenya: where it is working, where it is not, and what to do next. The evidence identifies uneven progress, concentrated risk, and a set of opportunities still open to act on. Every finding traces to a synthetic source record, every recommendation names an accountable owner, and every statistic in this publication is drawn from a single, internally consistent dataset.

report.subtitle: Decision intelligence for Public Service Delivery

branding.tagline: Every Voice. Every Language. Every Insight.
branding.synthetic_notice: Synthetic Demonstration Publication produced by VoiceInsights Africa. All people, quotations, locations and statistics are synthetic, internally governed demonstration content and are not official statistics.

report.executive_summary: What follows is a governed account of public service delivery performance in Kenya: where it is working, where it is not, and what to do next. The evidence identifies uneven progress, concentrated risk, and a set of opportunities still open to act on. Every finding traces to a synthetic source record, every recommendation names an accountable owner, and every statistic in this publication is drawn from a single, internally consistent dataset.

executive_book.executive_brief: Government leadership's attention should shift from monitoring public service delivery performance to acting on the specific groups and locations the evidence flags as most exposed.

decision_snapshot.recommendation: Issue a cabinet delivery directive with named accountabilities.
decision_snapshot.why_this_recommendation_exists: The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Nairobi Metro records a 82% signal for policy reach; the shortfall is concentrated among rural women, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Kenya, policy reach shows up hardest there, and rural women feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.
decision_snapshot.expected_benefit: A more consistent public service delivery experience for the groups currently furthest behind.
decision_snapshot.expected_risk: Weak cross-team coordination during early implementation
decision_snapshot.budget_requirement: Low — absorbable within existing allocations, subject to confirmation
decision_snapshot.budget_band: Low — absorbable within existing allocations, subject to confirmation
decision_snapshot.supporting_organization: M&E, Finance, Safeguarding and Delivery Units
decision_snapshot.monitoring_indicator: Milestone completion rate tracked against the DEC-GOV-01 delivery plan
decision_snapshot.success_criteria: A majority of milestones achieved, each independently verified against real evidence

decision_snapshot.why_this_recommendation_exists: Progress on administrative burden remains visible but not yet durable. The 83% result in Coast Counties weakens after disaggregation, particularly for young adults, suggesting that current gains depend on delivery conditions that remain fragile. The clearest signal in this dataset — though not the only one — is administrative burden in Coast Counties, where young adults sit furthest from parity.
decision_snapshot.expected_benefit: The evidence points toward improved public service delivery outcomes, though the size of the gain will depend on how consistently the action is resourced.
decision_snapshot.expected_risk: Likely — though not certain — erosion of momentum if the initial sponsor changes role
decision_snapshot.budget_requirement: Low — deliverable inside the current operating budget with light validation
decision_snapshot.budget_band: Low — deliverable inside the current operating budget with light validation
decision_snapshot.supporting_organization: M&E, Finance, Safeguarding and Delivery Units
decision_snapshot.monitoring_indicator: Proportion of agreed implementation steps verified on schedule for DEC-GOV-02
decision_snapshot.success_criteria: Milestone completion confirmed by evidence review, not self-reported progress

decision_snapshot.why_this_recommendation_exists: 84%. That is the county implementation variance signal recorded in Western Counties, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, persons with disabilities sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in Western Counties, concentrated among persons with disabilities. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.
decision_snapshot.expected_benefit: Reduced variation in public service delivery and a stronger evidence base for the next planning cycle.
decision_snapshot.expected_risk: Fragmented accountability once implementation moves beyond the pilot stage
dependencies: Stable staffing through the delivery window
decision_snapshot.budget_requirement: High — full business case and multi-year funding commitment required
decision_snapshot.budget_band: High — full business case and multi-year funding commitment required
decision_snapshot.supporting_organization: M&E, Finance, Safeguarding and Delivery Units
decision_snapshot.monitoring_indicator: Quarterly-verified progress against the milestones agreed for this critical-tier decision
decision_snapshot.success_criteria: Verified evidence of at least 80% delivery against the agreed milestone plan

critical_findings.title: Policy reach
critical_findings.text: The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Nairobi Metro records a 82% signal for policy reach; the shortfall is concentrated among rural women, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Kenya, policy reach shows up hardest there, and rural women feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.
critical_findings.interpretation: Triangulated across survey and interview evidence; strongest where baseline performance already lags.
critical_findings.headline: Policy reach is the binding constraint

critical_findings.title: Administrative burden
critical_findings.text: Progress on administrative burden remains visible but not yet durable. The 83% result in Coast Counties weakens after disaggregation, particularly for young adults, suggesting that current gains depend on delivery conditions that remain fragile. The clearest signal in this dataset — though not the only one — is administrative burden in Coast Counties, where young adults sit furthest from parity.
critical_findings.interpretation: The pattern appears consistent across evidence types, though a single additional data source would strengthen that confidence further.
critical_findings.headline: Delivery variation is masking the administrative burden gap

critical_findings.title: County implementation variance
critical_findings.text: 84%. That is the county implementation variance signal recorded in Western Counties, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, persons with disabilities sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in Western Counties, concentrated among persons with disabilities. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.
critical_findings.interpretation: Holds across independent evidence streams and intensifies in already under-resourced locations.
critical_findings.headline: County implementation variance gains remain exposed to reversal

critical_risks.risk: Leadership turnover during implementation stalls momentum on public service delivery
Coordination gaps between the teams responsible for public service delivery slow the pace of change

top_opportunities: Using the confidence intervals already published here to focus investment
Closing the equity gap the evidence has already located
Stronger feedback accountability between frontline delivery and leadership

priority_decisions.recommendation: Publish a citizen trust and service recovery dashboard.
priority_decisions.why_this_recommendation_exists: What would it take to fix citizen trust? Start with Arid and Semi-Arid Counties, where the signal sits at 85% and frontline workers carry most of the shortfall. The honest answer is sustained attention, not a single intervention. Arid and Semi-Arid Counties shows the sharpest divide, where frontline workers carry the largest share of the gap. Left unaddressed for a year, the imbalance is likely to erode equity, delivery confidence and the durability of results.
priority_decisions.expected_benefit: A stronger, more defensible case for public service delivery investment in the next planning cycle.
dependencies: Timely data collection for the next reporting cycle
Stable staffing through the delivery window

priority_decisions.why_this_recommendation_exists: Grievance resolution has become an accountability question rather than a measurement question. Evidence from Nairobi Metro places the signal at 86% and shows that low-income households experience the largest gap between policy intent and lived delivery. Of every study area examined in Kenya, grievance resolution shows up hardest there, and low-income households feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.

executive_book.budget_implications: The evidence points to a targeted reallocation within public service delivery, not a wholesale budget increase; a formal costing exercise would confirm the scale.
executive_book.cost_of_inaction: The synthetic evidence indicates that inaction on public service delivery would not simply preserve the status quo — it would let the weakest-performing groups fall further behind.
executive_book.executive_confidence: Confidence is high for what this synthetic demonstration is designed to show; not valid for real-world decision use.
executive_book.strategic_outlook: Twelve to eighteen months of consistent execution on public service delivery — not a single intervention — is what the evidence suggests these results need.
key_messages: The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Nairobi Metro records a 82% signal for policy reach; the shortfall is concentrated among rural women, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Kenya, policy reach shows up hardest there, and rural women feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.

Progress on administrative burden remains visible but not yet durable. The 83% result in Coast Counties weakens after disaggregation, particularly for young adults, suggesting that current gains depend on delivery conditions that remain fragile. The clearest signal in this dataset — though not the only one — is administrative burden in Coast Counties, where young adults sit furthest from parity.

84%. That is the county implementation variance signal recorded in Western Counties, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, persons with disabilities sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in Western Counties, concentrated among persons with disabilities. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.

What would it take to fix citizen trust? Start with Arid and Semi-Arid Counties, where the signal sits at 85% and frontline workers carry most of the shortfall. The honest answer is sustained attention, not a single intervention. Arid and Semi-Arid Counties shows the sharpest divide, where frontline workers carry the largest share of the gap. Left unaddressed for a year, the imbalance is likely to erode equity, delivery confidence and the durability of results.

Grievance resolution has become an accountability question rather than a measurement question. Evidence from Nairobi Metro places the signal at 86% and shows that low-income households experience the largest gap between policy intent and lived delivery. Of every study area examined in Kenya, grievance resolution shows up hardest there, and low-income households feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.

findings.text: Grievance resolution has become an accountability question rather than a measurement question. Evidence from Nairobi Metro places the signal at 86% and shows that low-income households experience the largest gap between policy intent and lived delivery. Of every study area examined in Kenya, grievance resolution shows up hardest there, and low-income households feel it most. findings.interpretation: The signal held up under cross-checking against independent evidence types, which is why it is treated here as decision-relevant rather than provisional.
findings.headline: The grievance resolution opportunity is actionable

statistical_intelligence.sampling_frame: Synthetic frame covering programme-relevant population groups in Kenya
statistical_intelligence.weighting: Post-stratification weights normalized to the synthetic population frame
statistical_intelligence.confidence_intervals: 95% confidence intervals shown for eligible weighted estimates
statistical_intelligence.regression: Multivariable regression included where outcome and assumptions are suitable

missing_data_analysis.treatment: multiple imputation for eligible analytical variables; complete-case sensitivity check

statistical_intelligence.data_quality_assessment: Passed synthetic consistency, range, duplicate and contradiction checks
statistical_intelligence.reproducibility_notes: Deterministic generator, documented transformations and export manifest
methodological_limitations: Synthetic data cannot support population inference

research_objectives: Assess public service delivery performance and equity
Identify operational drivers and decision priorities
evaluation_questions: Which actions are most likely to improve performance?
methodology.sampling_frame: Synthetic programme population frame for Kenya
methodology.metadata: Complete synthetic data dictionary and lineage registry

limitations: All content is synthetic and must not be interpreted as official statistics
Budget implications are directional and require formal costing
Causal claims are not made without an appropriate design

editors.reason: Every recommendation traces to real evidence.
Every finding traces to real evidence.
Every recommendation carries a real budget band and timeline.
Confidence intervals and design effect are disclosed.
Every recommendation has a named accountable owner.
Executive brief thesis is a single scannable statement.
No recommendation carries a redundant trailing sector clause.
The highest-priority decision has a named owner and a due window a Cabinet paper can cite.

executive_commentary.text: The wider implication follows directly from the tier: a high-priority decision now shapes what is realistically possible next reporting cycle.
For Permanent Secretary, this is not a monitoring item — it is a medium-tier decision due within 6–18 months.
The organisational implication is concrete: reduced variation in public service delivery and a stronger evidence base for the next planning cycle.
Left to routine channels, the exposure is real — execution capacity and coordination risk — which is why this sits above a standing agenda item.
What makes this an executive matter rather than an operational one is ownership: Permanent Secretary answers for the outcome, not only the process.

section_transitions: The evidence becomes clearer when read alongside what came before:
Taken together, these patterns matter more than any one figure alone:

regional.strongestImprovementRationale: Not available — this publication carries one measurement period per region, not a prior-period score to compare against.

strategic_interpretation_regional.whyItMatters: This is the specific signal a decision-maker should weight — not the aggregate figure alone.
strategic_interpretation_regional.decisionEnabled: Confirms whether the linked recommendation should proceed as prioritised or be re-sequenced.
strategic_interpretation_regional.uncertaintyRemaining: Differences between closely ranked regions may fall within sampling variation.
strategic_interpretation_regional.furtherEvidenceNeeded: A follow-up measurement in the next reporting cycle would confirm whether this is a sustained trend or a single-period reading.

quotes.quote: “Elsewhere, policy reach might be a footnote. For rural women in Nairobi Metro, it is the whole story.”
“Young adults in Coast Counties frame it simply: when administrative burden breaks down, everything else does too.”
“In Western Counties, county implementation variance shapes whether persons with disabilities can turn services into lasting outcomes.”
“Frontline workers in Arid and Semi-Arid Counties put it plainly: citizen trust is what decides whether progress holds or slips back.”
“Why does Nairobi Metro lag behind? Ask low-income households, and the answer comes back to grievance resolution.”
“Elsewhere, policy reach might be a footnote. For service managers in Coast Counties, it is the whole story.”
“Community leaders in Western Counties frame it simply: when administrative burden breaks down, everything else does too.”
“In Arid and Semi-Arid Counties, county implementation variance shapes whether private-sector partners can turn services into lasting outcomes.”
“Rural women in Nairobi Metro put it plainly: citizen trust is what decides whether progress holds or slips back.”
“Why does Coast Counties lag behind? Ask young adults, and the answer comes back to grievance resolution.”

framework_applicability.rationale: The publication uses governed response, evidence and export data.

The respondent base is 52% women nationally, with a real 3-point spread in women's participation share across regions — findings are read against gender-equitable participation, not assumed even by default.
Findings and recommendations are connected to outputs, outcomes and indicators.
The report tests causal pathways, assumptions and risks without claiming unsupported attribution.

publication_assurance.score_basis: Weighted evidence, statistical, visual, accessibility, export and human-review rules; never a field-count marketing score.

executive_book.executive_brief: What follows is a governed account of public service delivery performance in Kenya: where it is working, where it is not, and what to do next. The evidence identifies uneven progress, concentrated risk, and a set of opportunities still open to act on. Every finding traces to a synthetic source record, every recommendation names an accountable owner, and every statistic in this publication is drawn from a single, internally consistent dataset.

evidence_book.finding: The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Nairobi Metro records a 82% signal for policy reach; the shortfall is concentrated among rural women, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Kenya, policy reach shows up hardest there, and rural women feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.

Progress on administrative burden remains visible but not yet durable. The 83% result in Coast Counties weakens after disaggregation, particularly for young adults, suggesting that current gains depend on delivery conditions that remain fragile. The clearest signal in this dataset — though not the only one — is administrative burden in Coast Counties, where young adults sit furthest from parity.

84%. That is the county implementation variance signal recorded in Western Counties, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, persons with disabilities sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in Western Counties, concentrated among persons with disabilities. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.

What would it take to fix citizen trust? Start with Arid and Semi-Arid Counties, where the signal sits at 85% and frontline workers carry most of the shortfall. The honest answer is sustained attention, not a single intervention. Arid and Semi-Arid Counties shows the sharpest divide, where frontline workers carry the largest share of the gap. Left unaddressed for a year, the imbalance is likely to erode equity, delivery confidence and the durability of results.

Grievance resolution has become an accountability question rather than a measurement question. Evidence from Nairobi Metro places the signal at 86% and shows that low-income households experience the largest gap between policy intent and lived delivery. Of every study area examined in Kenya, grievance resolution shows up hardest there, and low-income households feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.

The clearest signal in this dataset — though not the only one — is administrative burden in Coast Counties, where young adults sit furthest from parity.

critical_risks.risk: Leadership turnover during implementation stalls momentum on public service delivery

Stronger feedback accountability between frontline delivery and leadership

priority_decisions.why_this_recommendation_exists: Grievance resolution has become an accountability question rather than a measurement question. Evidence from Nairobi Metro places the signal at 86% and shows that low-income households experience the largest gap between policy intent and lived delivery. Of every study area examined in Kenya, grievance resolution shows up hardest there, and low-income households feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.
priority_decisions.expected_benefit: Improved equity, delivery confidence and measurable public service delivery performance.

executive_book.budget_implications: The evidence points to a targeted reallocation within public service delivery, not a wholesale budget increase; a formal costing exercise would confirm the scale.
executive_book.cost_of_inaction: The synthetic evidence indicates that inaction on public service delivery would not simply preserve the status quo — it would let the weakest-performing groups fall further behind.
executive_book.executive_confidence: Confidence is high for what this synthetic demonstration is designed to show; not valid for real-world decision use.
executive_book.strategic_outlook: Twelve to eighteen months of consistent execution on public service delivery — not a single intervention — is what the evidence suggests these results need.
key_messages: The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Nairobi Metro records a 82% signal for policy reach; the shortfall is concentrated among rural women, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Kenya, policy reach shows up hardest there, and rural women feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.

Progress on administrative burden remains visible but not yet durable. The 83% result in Coast Counties weakens after disaggregation, particularly for young adults, suggesting that current gains depend on delivery conditions that remain fragile. The clearest signal in this dataset — though not the only one — is administrative burden in Coast Counties, where young adults sit furthest from parity.

84%. That is the county implementation variance signal recorded in Western Counties, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, persons with disabilities sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in Western Counties, concentrated among persons with disabilities. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.

What would it take to fix citizen trust? Start with Arid and Semi-Arid Counties, where the signal sits at 85% and frontline workers carry most of the shortfall. The honest answer is sustained attention, not a single intervention. Arid and Semi-Arid Counties shows the sharpest divide, where frontline workers carry the largest share of the gap. Left unaddressed for a year, the imbalance is likely to erode equity, delivery confidence and the durability of results.

Grievance resolution has become an accountability question rather than a measurement question. Evidence from Nairobi Metro places the signal at 86% and shows that low-income households experience the largest gap between policy intent and lived delivery. Of every study area examined in Kenya, grievance resolution shows up hardest there, and low-income households feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.

findings.title: Policy reach
findings.text: The aggregate result conceals a material implementation divide. Nairobi Metro records a 82% signal for policy reach; the shortfall is concentrated among rural women, where service continuity and confidence move together. Of every study area examined in Kenya, policy reach shows up hardest there, and rural women feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.
findings.interpretation: Triangulated across survey and interview evidence; strongest where baseline performance already lags.
findings.headline: Policy reach is the binding constraint

findings.title: Administrative burden
findings.text: Progress on administrative burden remains visible but not yet durable. The 83% result in Coast Counties weakens after disaggregation, particularly for young adults, suggesting that current gains depend on delivery conditions that remain fragile. The clearest signal in this dataset — though not the only one — is administrative burden in Coast Counties, where young adults sit furthest from parity.
findings.interpretation: The pattern appears consistent across evidence types, though a single additional data source would strengthen that confidence further.
findings.headline: Delivery variation is masking the administrative burden gap

findings.title: County implementation variance
findings.text: 84%. That is the county implementation variance signal recorded in Western Counties, and the headline figure understates the problem: disaggregated by group, persons with disabilities sit well below it. The clearest gap sits in Western Counties, concentrated among persons with disabilities. A twelve-month delay would let the gap compound into a harder, costlier problem.
findings.interpretation: Holds across independent evidence streams and intensifies in already under-resourced locations.
findings.headline: County implementation variance gains remain exposed to reversal

findings.title: Citizen trust
findings.text: What would it take to fix citizen trust? Start with Arid and Semi-Arid Counties, where the signal sits at 85% and frontline workers carry most of the shortfall. The honest answer is sustained attention, not a single intervention. Arid and Semi-Arid Counties shows the sharpest divide, where frontline workers carry the largest share of the gap. Left unaddressed for a year, the imbalance is likely to erode equity, delivery confidence and the durability of results.
findings.interpretation: Corroborated by multiple evidence types, concentrated where delivery capacity is already stretched.
findings.headline: Citizen trust now requires named accountability

findings.title: Grievance resolution
findings.text: Grievance resolution has become an accountability question rather than a measurement question. Evidence from Nairobi Metro places the signal at 86% and shows that low-income households experience the largest gap between policy intent and lived delivery. Of every study area examined in Kenya, grievance resolution shows up hardest there, and low-income households feel it most. A year of inaction would turn a gap into a pattern that is far harder to reverse.
findings.interpretation: The signal held up under cross-checking against independent evidence types, which is why it is treated here as decision-relevant rather than provisional.
findings.headline: The grievance resolution opportunity is actionable

editors.reason: Every recommendation traces to real evidence.
Every finding traces to real evidence.
Every recommendation carries a real budget band and timeline.
Confidence intervals and design effect are disclosed.
Every recommendation has a named accountable owner.
Executive brief thesis is a single scannable statement.
No recommendation carries a redundant trailing sector clause.
The highest-priority decision has a named owner and a due window a Cabinet paper can cite.

commonMisconceptions: That a policy being issued equals a policy being implemented
That citizen satisfaction reflects service reach evenly across regions
operationalRisks: Uneven implementation across counties or districts

executive_commentary.text: The wider implication follows directly from the tier: a high-priority decision now shapes what is realistically possible next reporting cycle.
For Permanent Secretary, this is not a monitoring item — it is a medium-tier decision due within 6–18 months.
The organisational implication is concrete: reduced variation in public service delivery and a stronger evidence base for the next planning cycle.
Left to routine channels, the exposure is real — execution capacity and coordination risk — which is why this sits above a standing agenda item.
What makes this an executive matter rather than an operational one is ownership: Permanent Secretary answers for the outcome, not only the process.

implications.description: An accountability mechanism is created where the evidence previously showed none.
Frontline delivery changes as a direct result, not only head-office practice.
This is grounded in a directive-level decision, the kind that typically requires formal instruction to take effect.

section_transitions: The evidence becomes clearer when read alongside what came before:
Taken together, these patterns matter more than any one figure alone:

regional.strongestImprovementRationale: Not available — this publication carries one measurement period per region, not a prior-period score to compare against.

strategic_interpretation_regional.whatChanged: Regional performance gap is measured at 3 points, with no prior comparison point available in this dataset.
strategic_interpretation_regional.whyItMatters: This is the specific signal a decision-maker should weight — not the aggregate figure alone.
strategic_interpretation_regional.decisionEnabled: Confirms whether the linked recommendation should proceed as prioritised or be re-sequenced.
strategic_interpretation_regional.uncertaintyRemaining: Differences between closely ranked regions may fall within sampling variation.
strategic_interpretation_regional.furtherEvidenceNeeded: A follow-up measurement in the next reporting cycle would confirm whether this is a sustained trend or a single-period reading.

strategic_interpretation_youth.whatChanged: Youth participation gap is measured at 3 points, with no prior comparison point available in this dataset.
strategic_interpretation_youth.whyItMatters: This is the specific signal a decision-maker should weight — not the aggregate figure alone.

dependencies.text: Shares its evidence base with DEC-GOV-02

spillover_effects.text: Progress or delay on this decision is likely to affect DEC-GOV-02, which draw on the same evidence.

unintended_consequences.text: Not estimable from this model — no second-order outcome data exists beyond the named risk.

cross_sector_implications.text: Not estimable from this model — this report does not carry cross-sector linkage data.

decision_options.rationale: Targeted reform (the recommendation as stated) scores highest (66%) among options that genuinely address the evidenced problem, under government-profile weighting, which weighs expected impact and feasibility and affordability and equity and institutional readiness and political feasibility most heavily.

verdicts.rationale: Requires cabinet-paper readiness (government_intelligence) and full editorial consensus.
Requires a named Ministry-level owner and complete budget guidance.
Requires disclosed confidence intervals and a stated budget-implications position.
Requires a Leave No One Behind-aligned standard and a real regional breakdown.
Requires every one of the donor-evaluation dimensions present, not a majority.
Requires valid knowledge routing plus Research Editor and Statistician consensus.
Requires at least one recommendation genuinely classified as a Board Decision and full editorial consensus — not satisfied by publications that are not board-owned.
Requires valid knowledge routing, disclosed evidence quality, and Research Editor consensus.

weaknesses.reason: Requires every one of the donor-evaluation dimensions present, not a majority.
Requires at least one recommendation genuinely classified as a Board Decision and full editorial consensus — not satisfied by publications that are not board-owned.

sdg_cards.title: Reduced Inequalities
sdg_cards.interpretation: The synthetic social and economic inclusion measure stands at 67% against a 91% planning threshold. The 24-point gap should be interpreted alongside disaggregated evidence and does not imply official SDG reporting or attribution.

sdg_cards.title: Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions
sdg_cards.interpretation: The synthetic satisfaction with public services measure stands at 68% against a 92% planning threshold. The 24-point gap should be interpreted alongside disaggregated evidence and does not imply official SDG reporting or attribution.

sdg_cards.title: Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions
sdg_cards.interpretation: The synthetic inclusive decision-making measure stands at 69% against a 76% planning threshold. The 7-point gap should be interpreted alongside disaggregated evidence and does not imply official SDG reporting or attribution.

framework_applicability.rationale: The publication uses governed response, evidence and export data.

The respondent base is 52% women nationally, with a real 3-point spread in women's participation share across regions — findings are read against gender-equitable participation, not assumed even by default.
Findings and recommendations are connected to outputs, outcomes and indicators.
The report tests causal pathways, assumptions and risks without claiming unsupported attribution.

publication_assurance.score_basis: Weighted evidence, statistical, visual, accessibility, export and human-review rules; never a field-count marketing score.

evidence_graph.evidence_chain: Dataset → Question Q1 → Respondent group → Finding 1 → Decision 1
evidence_graph.survey_question: Q1: How does policy reach affect public service delivery outcomes in Nairobi Metro?
evidence_graph.quote: “Elsewhere, policy reach might be a footnote. For rural women in Nairobi Metro, it is the whole story.”
evidence_graph.transcript_excerpt: Synthetic transcript 1: participants connected policy reach to uneven delivery, accountability and the need for locally owned follow-up.
